name: Platform-Neutral Access Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Initiate policy and technical scoping to draft a platform-neutral fallback concept note and procurement mapping. Systemic: 20% reduction in supplier-concentration risk, measured by a Diversification Index rising from 0.44 to 0.53, and a 6 percentage-point uplift in uptime during simulated platform outages (92% to 98%). Trade-off: resilience gains come with higher procurement complexity and longer cycles. Strategic: In the long term, strengthens Denmark's digital sovereignty and resilience, reducing vulnerability to platform-policymaking, but increases upfront investment and governance overhead.
options: Codify platform-neutral fallback requirements in AltID procurement and NSIS-aligned standards to systematically reduce vendor lock-in.
Prototype an open, standards-based fallback path in a multi-vendor testbed, integrating FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect, and hardware token support on GrapheneOS as baseline.
Adopt a self-sovereign identity model with decentralized identifiers and verifiable credentials, establishing a platform-agnostic trust fabric and a multi-issuer credential marketplace.
review: Controls platform dependence vs. speed of policy adoption. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy implications of self-sovereign identity and cross-border data minimization.

name: Procurement-Neutrality and Standards Pressure Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Publish revised procurement guidelines that embed platform-neutral interoperability criteria across AltID-related contracts. Systemic: 40% reduction in reliance on single-OS signals, measured by platform-dependent signals usage decreasing from 100% to 60%; Trade-off: procurement overhead and longer cycles. Strategic: Builds enduring resilience and sovereignty through diversified trust anchors, but requires sustained governance and administrative effort.
options: Codify procurement clauses that prioritize interoperability and platform neutrality in AltID tenders and long-term maintenance contracts.
Establish a cross-agency standards-review board to align NSIS, eIDAS, and EU interoperability goals and publish procurement-ready modular fallback specifications.
Form a multi-issuer standards consortium funded by government and partners to operationalize a shared, platform-agnostic trust infrastructure and open governance.
review: Controls procurement speed vs. depth of interoperability. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy governance and cross-border data minimization.

name: Demonstrator Execution Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Set up milestone-driven demonstration plans and secure cross-agency sign-offs. Systemic: 40% improvement in external validation credibility, measured by formal engagements with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and MitID increasing from 2 to 6; Trade-off: higher scope and cost. Strategic: Provides credible evidence to inform procurement language and EU alignment, but risks scope creep if ambitions widen beyond non-production demonstrations.
options: Publish phase-two demonstrator specifications for non-production mobile and browser-based fallbacks without live MitID integration.
Deliver two working non-production demonstrators (mobile-oriented and hardware-token-based fallback) and publicly document results and lessons learned.

Deploy a radical, cross-organization testbed with edge-enabled platform-neutral trust fabric using MPC-based credential verification and a distributed ledger for attestations, complemented by an open-cost maintenance model.
review: Controls demonstration scope vs. risk of scope creep. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy-preserving data handling in cross-organizational demonstrations.

name: Policy and Coalition-Building Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Launch policy outreach and publish initial policy notes to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen. Systemic: 35% increase in stakeholder alignment on platform-neutral goals, measured by Policy Alignment Index rising from 0.38 to 0.51; Trade-off: potential fragmentation of positions and slower consensus. Strategic: Increases Denmark’s influence in EU digital-identity policy but demands sustained political capital and resources.
options: Publish formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen emphasizing resilience, procurement neutrality, and supplier-diversification.
Build a cross-stakeholder coalition spanning civil society, privacy advocates, academia, and public-sector procurement to produce a joint platform-neutral advocacy plan and an engagement calendar.
Launch an international, EU-facing initiative to harmonize platform-neutral requirements and influence AltID-related EU guidance or Digital Identity Wallet materials.
review: Controls advocacy speed vs. policy depth. Weakness: The options fail to consider internal government procurement hurdles and budget cycles.

name: Continuity and Resilience Risk Management Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Draft risk registers and a continuity budget plan aligned with NSIS; schedule quarterly drills. Systemic: 25% reduction in expected downtime during platform disruptions, measured by MTTR decreasing from 8 hours to 6 hours; Trade-off: ongoing costs for insurance pools, attestations, and governance overhead. Strategic: Strengthens national resilience and sovereignty by hardening continuity capability, at the cost of higher recurring costs and administrative effort.
options: Establish formal risk registers and continuity playbooks aligned with NSIS, including quarterly drills and risk-based budget planning.
Implement a multi-party risk-sharing framework and a dedicated continuity fund to cover fallback-path maintenance and vendor diversification costs.
Create a platform-neutral, open-core trust fabric with continuous external security attestations and a public-private insurance pool to cover continuity risks and enable rapid reconfiguration.
review: Controls cost stability vs. resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy risk auditing and cross-border data flows for fallback paths.

name: Open-Standards Interoperability Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Begin policy and technical scoping to mandate open standards adoption across AltID components and fallback paths. Systemic: 30% increase in cross-vendor interoperability, measured by Standards Coverage Index rising from 0.40 to 0.58, and a 4 percentage-point uplift in platform-agnostic access during outages (90% to 94%). Trade-off: governance overhead and longer procurement cycles. Strategic: Over time, reduces vendor lock-in and enhances sovereignty, but requires sustained standards maintenance and governance.
options: Codify mandatory open-standards compliance in AltID procurement guidelines and NSIS-aligned interoperability criteria.
Create an open, multi-vendor testbed with shared baselines for FIDO2, WebAuthn, OpenID Connect, and hardware token support.
Institute a federated identity marketplace with open-source trust anchors and revenue-sharing for independent validators.
review: Controls vendor lock-in vs. interoperability acceleration. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy governance and cross-border data minimization.

name: Regulatory-Aligned Continuity Architecture Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Initiate formal continuity architecture reviews aligned with NSIS and eIDAS readiness. Systemic: 35% reduction in outage duration due to cross-border fallback architecture, measured by MTTR dropping from 8 hours to 5 hours; Trade-off: compliance overhead and governance burden. Strategic: Strengthens resilience and sovereignty, providing credible fallback but increasing ongoing regulatory overhead.
options: Mandate regulatory-aligned continuity architecture reviews within AltID procurement.
Develop cross-border mutual assistance agreements and fallback contracts with trusted providers.
Create a sovereign fallback chassis powered by a government-backed blockchain-based attestation network with smart contracts for SLAs and emergency re-routing.
review: Controls regulatory compliance vs. innovation agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy governance and cross-border data minimization.

name: Public-Procurement Engagement Strategy for Platform-Neutrality
consequences: Immediate: Launch procurement outreach and publish initial guidelines. Systemic: 40% increase in procurement agility, measured by cycle time reduction from 12 months to 7 months; 20% reduction in vendor lock-in due to multi-supplier pilots. Trade-off: risk of scope creep and negotiation complexity. Strategic: Improves market resilience and reduces lock-in risk but demands governance oversight.
options: Publish a procurement playbook with anti-lock-in KPIs and vendor-neutral evaluation criteria.
Institute a procurement sandbox and multi-supplier pilots with staged funding and clear exit criteria.
Launch a government-backed platform-neutral identity marketplace with dynamic, outcome-based contracts and open-source compliance tooling.
review: Controls procurement speed vs. quality/standardization. Weakness: The options fail to consider internal government procurement hurdles and budget cycles.

name: Resilience Measurement and Independent Audit Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Define resilience metrics dashboard and schedule external attestations. Systemic: 30% reduction in incident response time, measured by MTTR from 6 hours to 4 hours; 15-point increase in public trust due to transparent reporting (Trust Index from 0.42 to 0.57). Trade-off: ongoing audit costs. Strategic: Establishes credibility and public trust but increases recurring costs.
options: Define resilience metrics dashboard and publish quarterly performance reports.
Commission independent security attestations and publish results in EU compliance portals.
Pilot continuous assurance via real-time attestation tokens and a crowdsourced monitoring system with token-curated registries.
review: Controls audit cost vs. continuous assurance. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy risk auditing and cross-border data flows for fallback paths.

name: Privacy-By-Design and Cross-Border Governance Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Embed privacy-by-design across the fallback architecture and enforce data minimization. Systemic: 40% reduction in data exposure risk across cross-border flows, measured by a data-exposure index dropping from 0.25 to 0.15; Trade-off: higher upfront design effort and potential performance overhead. Strategic: Improves GDPR-aligned trust and public acceptance but increases initial program cost.
options: Adopt privacy-by-design principles across fallback architecture and ensure data minimization.
Implement data governance agreements for cross-border flows and privacy impact assessments across fallback paths.
Launch a privacy-preserving identity framework using zero-knowledge proofs and selective disclosure with cross-border governance.
review: Controls privacy protection vs. performance in fallback flows. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border data flows and data minimization.

name: Federated Validator Ecosystem Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Establish governance scaffolding for a federated validator network and cross-provider attestation framework. → Systemic: 25% reduction in supplier concentration risk, measured by a Vendor-Diversity score rising from 0.42 to 0.58, and a 3 percentage-point uplift in platform availability during outages (92% → 95%). → Strategic: Long-term sovereignty and resilience improved, but governance overhead and coordination friction rise. Trade-off: Centralization of trust decisions vs. governance overhead.
options: Establish a government-backed federated validator network with shared attestation standards and cross-border capability.
Create a multi-vendor validator framework with open-source baselines and transparent audits across partners.
Launch a radical decentralized identity marketplace with validator staking, tokenized governance, and DAO-led attestation oversight.
review: Controls vendor concentration risk vs. governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy governance and cross-border data minimization.

name: Hardware-Token Interoperability and Biometrics Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Align procurement to require cross-vendor hardware-token interoperability and WebAuthn readiness. → Systemic: 40% reduction in platform-token lock-in risk, measured by token-ecosystem diversity rising from 0.35 to 0.53; 5 percentage-point uplift in authentication success during constrained sessions (90% → 95%). → Strategic: Long-term resilience and user autonomy increase, but hardware standardization increases procurement cycle times and adds governance overhead. Trade-off: user convenience vs governance and supply chain complexity.
options: Mandate hardware-token-first fallback across AltID components and promote cross-vendor token interoperability.
Pilot biometrics-based adaptive authentication on privacy-respecting devices with privacy-preserving techniques.
Launch radical hardware-token ecosystem with an open hardware standard and a token-as-a-service model, plus distributed attestation.
review: Controls token strategy durability vs privacy risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability with EU-level privacy and cross-border data flows.

name: EU-Identity Governance and Local Autonomy Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Initiate EU-alignment reviews while preserving Denmark's local procurement autonomy. → Systemic: 35% improvement in cross-border interoperability readiness, measured by a Denmark-EU alignment score rising from 0.45 to 0.60; 3-point improvement in time-to-adoption for EU requirements. → Strategic: Interoperability increases portability but may constrain domestic tailoring and elevate administrative load. Trade-off: EU alignment speed vs. local autonomy.
options: Formalize a Denmark-EU governance compact to harmonize eIDAS2 expectations.
Develop a modular autonomy plan to integrate EU wallet guidelines with national procurement choices.
Launch radical EU-Denmark platform-neutral core and cross-border fallback hub financed by a shared fund and governed by a cross-state DAO.
review: Controls EU alignment vs. local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider cost and timeline pressures on smaller municipalities.

name: Open-Source Core Identity and Validation Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Move critical identity components to permissively licensed open-source cores and require external audits. → Systemic: 60% reduction in proprietary lock-in risk, measured by open-source footprint of critical components rising from 20% to 60%; 6-point improvement in external validation effectiveness. → Strategic: Long-term resilience via community governance, but ongoing open-source maintenance costs and potential license compatibility friction. Trade-off: independence vs maintenance burden.
options: Move critical fallback components to openly licensed, auditable core with transparent governance.
Develop a shared open-source validator stack with cross-agency collaboration and public audits.
Launch radical open-source platform-neutral fallback fabric governed by a global community and funded by a revenue-sharing model for validators.
review: Controls openness vs maintenance costs. Weakness: The options fail to consider licensing and long-term sustainability of open-source components.

name: Post-Quantum Readiness and Resilience Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Begin PQC readiness program and plan migration of cryptographic suites. → Systemic: 25% reduction in quantum-risk exposure across identity flows, measured by a risk index dropping from 0.40 to 0.30; 15% longer key lifetimes with PQC adoption. → Strategic: Strengthens forward security and regulatory alignment, but increases deployment complexity and cross-vendor coordination. Trade-off: forward security vs integration complexity.
options: Adopt PQC-ready cryptographic primitives in fallback stacks with a staged migration plan.
Run non-production PQC pilots to validate performance and interoperability with EU standards.
Launch radical post-quantum identity fabric using quantum-safe hardware modules and cross-border governance protocol.
review: Controls quantum-readiness vs deployment complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory approvals for emerging cryptographic techniques.
